name: 黄祖耀

work_experience.position: Worked in family commodity trading (rubber, pepper, sago flour)

work_experience.organization: United Chinese Bank / United Overseas Bank (UOB)

work_experience.position: Chairman (stepped down as CEO; son Wee Ee Cheong succeeded as CEO)

person_relationships.description: Father; founder of United Chinese Bank (now UOB) in 1935 and businessman
Eldest son; succeeded as CEO and Deputy Chairman of UOB in 2007; billionaire
Founder of Overseas Union Bank (OUB); Wee convinced the Lien family to sell OUB to UOB in 2001

political_stances.stance_content: Critic of the Singapore government's plan to liberalize the banking sector and allow foreign banks more market access; restructured UOB operations to meet increased competition

major_achievements.achievement: Grew UOB's trade financing business more than a hundredfold; renamed United Chinese Bank to UOB for Hong Kong expansion
Acquired 49.8% of Chung Khiaw Bank for S$22 million, doubling UOB's size and creating Singapore and Malaysia's second-largest banking group
Listed UOB on the Hong Kong exchange and acquired Lee Wah Bank

major_achievements.organization: Singapore Federation of Chinese Clan Associations (SFCCA)
major_achievements.achievement: Founding President of SFCCA, an umbrella group for 190 Chinese clan associations in Singapore; served until 2010
Named Singapore's Businessman of the Year by The Business Times
Prime mover in formation of CDAC; became Chairman of its Board of Trustees
Named ASEAN Businessman of the Year by the ASEAN Business Forum
Acquired Overseas Union Bank (OUB) in a S$10 billion cash-and-shares deal, outbidding government-linked DBS Bank
Named Singapore's Businessman of the Year by The Business Times for the second time
Received the inaugural Credit Suisse-Ernst & Young Lifetime Achievement Award for pioneering work in Singapore's financial industry
Received an honorary doctorate from NUS
Awarded The Asian Banker Lifetime Achievement Award for contributions to the banking industry and broader community
Listed by Forbes as Singapore's wealthiest individual with a net worth of US$4.2 billion

biography_summary: Wee Cho Yaw DUBC BBM (10 January 1929 - 3 February 2024) was a Singaporean banker, businessman, investor, and philanthropist who built United Overseas Bank (UOB) into one of Southeast Asia's largest financial institutions. Born in Kinmen, Fujian, China, he fled to Borneo and then Singapore during the Sino-Japanese War. He joined the board of United Chinese Bank (later UOB) in 1958 at age 29, became Managing Director in 1960, and succeeded his father Wee Kheng Chiang as Chairman in 1974. Over six decades, he transformed UOB through strategic acquisitions including Chung Khiaw Bank (1971), Lee Wah Bank (1972), Far Eastern Bank, Industrial and Commercial Bank, and the landmark S$10 billion acquisition of Overseas Union Bank (2001). He was also founding president of the Singapore Federation of Chinese Clan Associations (1985-2010), headed the Hokkien Huay Kuan (1972-2010), chaired the Nanyang University Council, and was Pro-Chancellor of NTU from 2004. He established the Wee Foundation in 2009 with a S$30 million endowment for education and welfare. His eldest son Wee Ee Cheong succeeded him as UOB CEO. At the time of his death, his net worth was estimated at US$9.47 billion.

collection_meta.notes: Comprehensive enrichment from Wikipedia, NLB, Forbes, and Straits Times. Added full career timeline, all major acquisitions, family members (wife Chuang Yong Eng, 3 sons, 2 daughters), philanthropy (Wee Foundation), and community leadership roles. Birth place corrected to Kinmen, Fujian. Death date 2024-02-03 confirmed.
